sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
[Waking up] Help me, Lysander, help me! Do thy best
To pluck this crawling serpent from my breast.
Ay me, for pity! What a dream was here.
Lysander, look how I do quake with fear.
Methought a serpent eat my heart away,
And you sat smiling at his cruel pray.
Lysander!—What, removed?—Lysander, lord!—
What, out of hearing, gone? No sound, no word?—
Alack, where are you? Speak, an if you hear.
Speak, of all loves! I swoon almost with fear.
No? Then I well perceive you all not nigh.
Either death or you I'll find immediately.

And you sat smiling at his cruel pray.
Lysander!—What, removed?—Lysander, lord!—
What, out of hearing, gone? No sound, no word?—
Alack, where are you? Speak, an if you hear.
Speak, of all loves! I swoon almost with fear.
No? Then I well perceive you all not nigh.
Either death or you I'll find immediately.

While TITANIA sleeps onstage, BOTTOM, QUINCE, FLUTE, SNUG, SNOUT, and STARVELING enter.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text: Are we all met?

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
Pat, pat. And here’s a marvelous convenient place for
our rehearsal. This green plot shall be our stage, this
hawthorn-brake our tiring-house, and we will do it in
action as we will do it before the duke.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text: Peter Quince.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: What sayest thou, bully Bottom?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
There are things in this comedy of Pyramus and Thisbe
that will never please. First, Pyramus must draw a sword
to kill himself, which the ladies cannot abide. How
answer you that?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
There are things in this comedy of Pyramus and Thisbe
that will never please. First, Pyramus must draw a sword
to kill himself, which the ladies cannot abide. How
answer you that?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
There are things in this comedy of Pyramus and Thisbe
that will never please. First, Pyramus must draw a sword
to kill himself, which the ladies cannot abide. How
answer you that?

sentences.speaker: SNOUT
sentences.text: By 'r lakin, a parlous fear.

sentences.speaker: STARVELING
sentences.text:
I believe we must leave the killing out, when all is
done.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Not a whit. I have a device to make all well. Write me
a prologue, and let the prologue seem to say we will do
no harm with our swords, and that Pyramus is not killed
indeed. And for the more better assurance, tell them
that I, Pyramus, am not Pyramus, but Bottom the weaver.
This will put them out of fear.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
Well. We will have such a prologue, and it shall be
written in eight and six.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:

written in eight and six.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
No, make it two more. Let it be written in eight and
eight.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
No, make it two more. Let it be written in eight and
eight.

sentences.speaker: SNOUT
sentences.text: Will not the ladies be afeard of the lion?

sentences.speaker: STARVELING
sentences.text: I fear it, I promise you.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Masters, you ought to consider with yourselves. To
bring in—God shield us!—a lion among ladies is a most
dreadful thing. For there is not a more fearful wildfowl
than your lion living. And we ought to look to ’t.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:

dreadful thing. For there is not a more fearful wildfowl
than your lion living. And we ought to look to ’t.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Masters, you ought to consider with yourselves. To

sentences.speaker: SNOUT
sentences.text: Therefore another prologue must tell he is not a lion.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Nay, you must name his name, and half his face must be
seen through the lion’s neck. And he himself must speak
through, saying thus—or to the same defect—“Ladies,” or
“Fair ladies,” “I would wish you” or “I would request
you” or “I would entreat you” “not to fear, not to
tremble, my life for yours. If you think I come hither
as a lion, it were pity of my life. No, I am no such
thing. I am a man as other men are.” And there indeed
let him name his name, and tell them plainly he is Snug
the joiner.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
Well, it shall be so. But there is two hard things:
that is, to bring the moonlight into a chamber. For, you
know, Pyramus and Thisbe meet by moonlight.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:

know, Pyramus and Thisbe meet by moonlight.

sentences.speaker: SNOUT
sentences.text: Doth the moon shine that night we play our play?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
A calendar, a calendar! Look in the almanac. Find out
moonshine, find out moonshine!

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
A calendar, a calendar! Look in the almanac. Find out
moonshine, find out moonshine!

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
A calendar, a calendar! Look in the almanac. Find out
moonshine, find out moonshine!

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: [Takes out a book] Yes, it doth shine that night.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Why then, may you leave a casement of the great chamber
window where we play open, and the moon may shine in at
the casement.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
Ay. Or else one must come in with a bush of thorns and
a lantern, and say he comes to disfigure, or to present,
the person of Moonshine. Then, there is another thing:
we must have a wall in the great chamber. For Pyramus
and Thisbe, says the story, did talk through the chink
of a wall.

sentences.speaker: SNOUT
sentences.text: You can never bring in a wall. What say you, Bottom?

sentences.speaker: SNOUT
sentences.text: You can never bring in a wall. What say you, Bottom?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Some man or other must present Wall. And let him have
some plaster, or some loam, or some roughcast about him
to signify wall. And let him hold his fingers thus, and
through that cranny shall Pyramus and Thisbe whisper.

If that may be then all is well. Come, sit down, every
mother’s son, and rehearse your parts. Pyramus, you
begin. When you have spoken your speech, enter into that
brake. And so everyone according to his cue.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
[Aside] What hempen homespuns have we swaggering here,
So near the cradle of the fairy queen?
What, a play toward? I’ll be an auditor.
An actor too, perhaps, if I see cause.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Speak, Pyramus. Thisbe, stand forth.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Speak, Pyramus. Thisbe, stand forth.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
[As PYRAMUS] Thisbe, the flowers of odious savors
sweet—

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: “Odors,” “odors.”

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
[As PYRAMUS]
—odors savors sweet,
So hath thy breath, my dearest Thisbe dear.
But hark, a voice! Stay thou but here awhile,
And by and by I will to thee appear.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text: [Aside] A stranger Pyramus than e'er played here.

sentences.speaker: FLUTE
sentences.text: Must I speak now?
ROBIN exits.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
Ay, marry, must you. For you must understand he goes
but to see a noise that he heard, and is to come again.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
Ay, marry, must you. For you must understand he goes
but to see a noise that he heard, and is to come again.

sentences.speaker: FLUTE
sentences.text:
[As THISBE] Most radiant Pyramus, most lily-white of
hue,
Of color like the red rose on triumphant brier,
Most brisky juvenal and eke most lovely Jew,
As true as truest horse that yet would never tire.
I’ll meet thee, Pyramus, at Ninny’s tomb.

sentences.text:

hue,

I’ll meet thee, Pyramus, at Ninny’s tomb.

“Ninus' tomb,” man. Why, you must not speak that yet.
That you answer to Pyramus. You speak all your part at
once, cues and all. Pyramus, enter. Your cue is past. It
is “never tire.”

masters! Fly, masters! Help!

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
I’ll follow you. I’ll lead you about a round
Through bog, through bush, through brake, through
brier.
Sometime a horse I’ll be, sometime a hound,
A hog, a headless bear, sometime a fire.
And neigh, and bark, and grunt, and roar, and burn,
Like horse, hound, hog, bear, fire, at every turn.

Sometime a horse I’ll be, sometime a hound,
A hog, a headless bear, sometime a fire.
And neigh, and bark, and grunt, and roar, and burn,
Like horse, hound, hog, bear, fire, at every turn.
ROBIN exits.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Why do they run away? This is a knavery of them to make
me afeard.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Why do they run away? This is a knavery of them to make
me afeard.
SNOUT enters.

sentences.speaker: SNOUT
sentences.text: O Bottom, thou art changed! What do I see on thee?

sentences.speaker: SNOUT
sentences.text: O Bottom, thou art changed! What do I see on thee?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
What do you see? You see an ass head of your own, do
you?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
What do you see? You see an ass head of your own, do
you?
SNOUT exits. QUINCE enters.
SNOUT exits. QUINCE enters.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Bless thee, Bottom, bless thee. Thou art translated.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Bless thee, Bottom, bless thee. Thou art translated.
QUINCE exits.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
I see their knavery: this is to make an ass of me, to
fright me if they could. But I will not stir from this
place, do what they can. I will walk up and down here
and I will sing, that they shall hear I am not afraid.
[Sings]
The ouzel cock, so black of hue
With orange-tawny bill,
The throstlewith his note so true,
The wren with little quill—

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text: [Waking] What angel wakes me from my flowery bed?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
[Sings]
The finch, the sparrow, and the lark,
The plainsong cuckoo gray,
Whose note full many a man doth mark
And dares not answer “Nay”—
For indeed, who would set his wit to so foolish a bird?
Who would give a bird the lie, though he cry “cuckoo”
never so?

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
I pray thee, gentle mortal, sing again.
Mine ear is much enamored of thy note.
So is mine eye enthrallèd to thy shape.
And thy fair virtue’s force perforce doth move me
On the first view to say, to swear, I love thee.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Methinks, mistress, you should have little reason for
that. And yet, to say the truth, reason and love keep
little company together nowadays. The more the pity that
some honest neighbors will not make them friends. Nay,
I can gleek upon occasion.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text: Thou art as wise as thou art beautiful.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Not so, neither. But if I had wit enough to get out of
this wood, I have enough to serve mine own turn.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Not so, neither. But if I had wit enough to get out of
this wood, I have enough to serve mine own turn.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
Out of this wood do not desire to go.
Thou shalt remain here whether thou wilt or no.
I am a spirit of no common rate.
The summer still doth tend upon my state.
And I do love thee. Therefore go with me.
I’ll give thee fairies to attend on thee.
And they shall fetch thee jewels from the deep,
And sing while thou on pressèd flowers dost sleep.
And I will purge thy mortal grossness so
That thou shalt like an airy spirit go.
Peaseblossom, Cobweb, Moth, and Mustardseed!

sentences.speaker: PEASEBLOSSOM

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: Where shall we go?

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
Be kind and courteous to this gentleman.
Hop in his walks and gambol in his eyes.
Feed him with apricoks and dewberries,
With purple grapes, green figs, and mulberries.
The honey bags steal from the humble-bees,
And for night tapers crop their waxen thighs
And light them at the fiery glowworms' eyes
To have my love to bed and to arise.
And pluck the wings from painted butterflies
To fan the moonbeams from his sleeping eyes.
Nod to him, elves, and do him courtesies.

sentences.speaker: MUSTARDSEED
sentences.text: Mustardseed.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Good Master Mustardseed, I know your patience well.
That same cowardly, giantlike ox-beef hath devoured many
a gentleman of your house. I promise you your kindred
hath made my eyes water ere now. I desire you of more
acquaintance, good Master Mustardseed.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
Come, wait upon him. Lead him to my bower.
The moon methinks looks with a watery eye.
And when she weeps, weeps every little flower,
Lamenting some enforcèd chastity.
Tie up my love's tongue. Bring him silently.

They exit.
The Fairy King OBERON enters.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
I wonder if Titania be awaked.
Then, what it was that next came in her eye,
Which she must dote on in extremity.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
I wonder if Titania be awaked.
Then, what it was that next came in her eye,
Which she must dote on in extremity.
ROBIN enters.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Here comes my messenger.
How now, mad spirit?
What night-rule now about this haunted grove?

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Here comes my messenger.
How now, mad spirit?
What night-rule now about this haunted grove?

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Here comes my messenger.
How now, mad spirit?
What night-rule now about this haunted grove?

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
My mistress with a monster is in love.
Near to her close and consecrated bower,
While she was in her dull and sleeping hour,
A crew of patches, rude mechanicals
That work for bread upon Athenian stalls,
Were met together to rehearse a play
Intended for great Theseus' nuptial day.
The shallowest thick-skin of that barren sort,
Who Pyramus presented in their sport,
Forsook his scene and entered in a brake,
When I did him at this advantage take,
An ass’s nole I fixèd on his head.
Anon his Thisbe must be answerèd,
And forth my mimic comes. When they him spy,
As wild geese that the creeping fowler eye,
Or russet-pated choughs, many in sort,
Rising and cawing at the gun’s report,
Sever themselves and madly sweep the sky,
So at his sight away his fellows fly;
And, at our stamp, here o'er and o'er one falls.
He “Murder!” cries and help from Athens calls.
Their sense thus weak, lost with their fears thus
strong,
Made senseless things begin to do them wrong.
For briers and thorns at their apparel snatch,
Some sleeves, some hats—from yielders all things catch.
I led them on in this distracted fear
And left sweet Pyramus translated there.
When in that moment so it came to pass,
Titania waked and straightway loved an ass.

Anon his Thisbe must be answerèd,
And forth my mimic comes. When they him spy,
As wild geese that the creeping fowler eye,
Or russet-pated choughs, many in sort,
Rising and cawing at the gun’s report,
Sever themselves and madly sweep the sky,
So at his sight away his fellows fly;
And, at our stamp, here o'er and o'er one falls.
He “Murder!” cries and help from Athens calls.
Their sense thus weak, lost with their fears thus
strong,
Made senseless things begin to do them wrong.
For briers and thorns at their apparel snatch,
Some sleeves, some hats—from yielders all things catch.
I led them on in this distracted fear
And left sweet Pyramus translated there.
When in that moment so it came to pass,
Titania waked and straightway loved an ass.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
This falls out better than I could devise.
But hast thou yet latched the Athenian’s eyes
With the love juice, as I did bid thee do?

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
This falls out better than I could devise.
But hast thou yet latched the Athenian’s eyes
With the love juice, as I did bid thee do?

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
I took him sleeping—that is finished too—
And the Athenian woman by his side,
That, when he waked, of force she must be eyed.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
I took him sleeping—that is finished too—
And the Athenian woman by his side,
That, when he waked, of force she must be eyed.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
I took him sleeping—that is finished too—
And the Athenian woman by his side,
That, when he waked, of force she must be eyed.
DEMETRIUS and HERMIA enter.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
[Aside to ROBIN] Stand close. This is the same
Athenian.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
[Aside to ROBIN] Stand close. This is the same
Athenian.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
[Aside to OBERON] This is the woman, but not this the
man.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Oh, why rebuke you him that loves you so?
Lay breath so bitter on your bitter foe.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Oh, why rebuke you him that loves you so?
Lay breath so bitter on your bitter foe.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
Now I but chide, but I should use thee worse.
For thou, I fear, hast given me cause to curse.
If thou hast slain Lysander in his sleep,
Being o'er shoes in blood, plunge in the deep,
And kill me too.
The sun was not so true unto the day
As he to me. Would he have stolen away
From sleeping Hermia? I’ll believe as soon
This whole Earth may be bored, and that the moon
May through the center creep and so displease
Her brother’s noontide with th' Antipodes.
It cannot be but thou hast murdered him.
So should a murderer look, so dead, so grim.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
So should the murdered look, and so should I,
Pierced through the heart with your stern cruelty.
Yet you, the murderer, look as bright, as clear,
As yonder Venus in her glimmering sphere.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
What’s this to my Lysander? Where is he?
Ah, good Demetrius, wilt thou give him me?

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
What’s this to my Lysander? Where is he?
Ah, good Demetrius, wilt thou give him me?

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
What’s this to my Lysander? Where is he?
Ah, good Demetrius, wilt thou give him me?

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: I had rather give his carcass to my hounds.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
Out, dog! Out, cur! Thou drivest me past the bounds
Of maiden’s patience. Hast thou slain him then?
Henceforth be never numbered among men!
Oh, once tell true, tell true even for my sake—
Durst thou have looked upon him being awake,
And hast thou killed him sleeping? O brave touch!
Could not a worm, an adder, do so much?
An adder did it, for with doubler tongue
Than thine, thou serpent, never adder stung.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
You spend your passion on a misprised mood.
I am not guilty of Lysander’s blood.
Nor is he dead, for aught that I can tell.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
You spend your passion on a misprised mood.
I am not guilty of Lysander’s blood.
Nor is he dead, for aught that I can tell.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:

I am not guilty of Lysander’s blood.
Nor is he dead, for aught that I can tell.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: I pray thee, tell me then that he is well.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: An if I could, what should I get therefore?

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
A privilege never to see me more.
And from thy hated presence part I so.
See me no more, whether he be dead or no.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
A privilege never to see me more.
And from thy hated presence part I so.
See me no more, whether he be dead or no.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
A privilege never to see me more.
And from thy hated presence part I so.
See me no more, whether he be dead or no.
HERMIA exits.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
There is no following her in this fierce vein.
Here therefore for a while I will remain.
So sorrow’s heaviness doth heavier grow
For debt that bankrupt sleep doth sorrow owe,
Which now in some slight measure it will pay,
If for his tender here I make some stay.
[Lies down and sleeps]

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
[To ROBIN] What hast thou done? Thou hast mistaken
quite,
And laid the love juice on some true love’s sight.
Of thy misprision must perforce ensue
Some true love turned, and not a false turned true.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
Then fate o'errules that, one man holding troth,
A million fail, confounding oath on oath.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
Then fate o'errules that, one man holding troth,
A million fail, confounding oath on oath.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
About the wood go swifter than the wind,
And Helena of Athens look thou find—
All fancy-sick she is and pale of cheer,
With sighs of love, that costs the fresh blood dear.
By some illusion see thou bring her here.

I go, I go. Look how I go,
Swifter than arrow from the Tartar’s bow.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
[Squeezing flower juice into DEMETRIUS ’s eyes]
Flower of this purple dye,
Hit with Cupid’s archery,
Sink in apple of his eye.
When his love he doth espy,
Let her shine as gloriously
As the Venus of the sky.
When thou wakest, if she be by,
Beg of her for remedy.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
Captain of our fairy band,
Helena is here at hand,
And the youth, mistook by me,
Pleading for a lover’s fee.
Shall we their fond pageant see?
Lord, what fools these mortals be!

Stand aside. The noise they make
Will cause Demetrius to awake.

sentences.speaker: OBERON

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
Then will two at once woo one.
That must needs be sport alone.
And those things do best please me
That befall preposterously.

LYSANDER and HELENA enter.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
Why should you think that I should woo in scorn?
Scorn and derision never come in tears.
Look, when I vow, I weep. And vows so born,
In their nativity all truth appears.
How can these things in me seem scorn to you,
Bearing the badge of faith to prove them true?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
You do advance your cunning more and more.
When truth kills truth, O devilish holy fray!
These vows are Hermia’s. Will you give her o'er?
Weigh oath with oath, and you will nothing weigh.
Your vows to her and me, put in two scales,
Will even weigh, and both as light as tales.

sentences.text: I had no judgment when to her I swore.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Nor none, in my mind, now you give her o'er.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: Demetrius loves her, and he loves not you.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
[Waking] O Helena, goddess, nymph, perfect, divine!
To what, my love, shall I compare thine eyne?
Crystal is muddy. Oh, how ripe in show
Thy lips, those kissing cherries, tempting grow!
That pure congealèd white, high Taurus' snow,
Fanned with the eastern wind, turns to a crow
When thou hold’st up thy hand. Oh, let me kiss
This princess of pure white, this seal of bliss!

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
O spite! O hell! I see you all are bent
To set against me for your merriment.
If you were civil and knew courtesy,
You would not do me thus much injury.
Can you not hate me, as I know you do,
But you must join in souls to mock me too?
If you were men, as men you are in show,
You would not use a gentle lady so
To vow, and swear, and superpraise my parts,
When I am sure you hate me with your hearts.
You both are rivals, and love Hermia,
And now both rivals to mock Helena—
A trim exploit, a manly enterprise,
To conjure tears up in a poor maid’s eyes
With your derision! None of noble sort
Would so offend a virgin, and extort
A poor soul’s patience, all to make you sport.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
You are unkind, Demetrius. Be not so.
For you love Hermia. This you know I know.
And here, with all good will, with all my heart,
In Hermia’s love I yield you up my part.
And yours of Helena to me bequeath,
Whom I do love and will do till my death.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Never did mockers waste more idle breath.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Lysander, keep thy Hermia. I will none.
If e'er I loved her, all that love is gone.
My heart to her but as guest-wise sojourned,
And now to Helen is it home returned,
There to remain.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Disparage not the faith thou dost not know,
Lest to thy peril thou aby it dear.
Look, where thy love comes. Yonder is thy dear.

HERMIA enters.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
Dark night, that from the eye his function takes,
The ear more quick of apprehension makes.
Wherein it doth impair the seeing sense,
It pays the hearing double recompense.
Thou art not by mine eye, Lysander, found.
Mine ear, I thank it, brought me to thy sound
But why unkindly didst thou leave me so?

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: What love could press Lysander from my side?

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
Lysander’s love, that would not let him bide,
Fair Helena, who more engilds the night
Than all yon fiery oes and eyes of light.
Why seek’st thou me? Could not this make thee know
The hate I bear thee made me leave thee so?

sentences.text: You speak not as you think. It cannot be.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Lo, she is one of this confederacy!
Now I perceive they have conjoined all three
To fashion this false sport, in spite of me.
Injurious Hermia! Most ungrateful maid!
Have you conspired, have you with these contrived
To bait me with this foul derision?
Is all the counsel that we two have shared,
The sisters' vows, the hours that we have spent
When we have chid the hasty-footed time
For parting us—oh, is it all forgot?
All schooldays' friendship, childhood innocence?
We, Hermia, like two artificial gods,
Have with our needles created both one flower,
Both on one sampler, sitting on one cushion,
Both warbling of one song, both in one key,
As if our hands, our sides, voices, and minds,
Had been incorporate. So we grew together,
Like to a double cherry—seeming parted
But yet an union in partition—
Two lovely berries molded on one stem;

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
I am amazèd at your passionate words.
I scorn you not. It seems that you scorn me.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
I am amazèd at your passionate words.
I scorn you not. It seems that you scorn me.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
I am amazèd at your passionate words.
I scorn you not. It seems that you scorn me.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Have you not set Lysander, as in scorn,
To follow me and praise my eyes and face?
And made your other love, Demetrius—
Who even but now did spurn me with his foot—
To call me goddess, nymph, divine, and rare,
Precious, celestial? Wherefore speaks he this
To her he hates? And wherefore doth Lysander
Deny your love, so rich within his soul,
And tender me, forsooth, affection,
But by your setting on, by your consent?
What though I be not so in grace as you—
So hung upon with love, so fortunate—
But miserable most, to love unloved?
This you should pity rather than despise.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: I understand not what you mean by this.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Ay, do. Persever, counterfeit sad looks,
Make mouths upon me when I turn my back,
Wink each at other, hold the sweet jest up—
This sport, well carried, shall be chronicled.
If you have any pity, grace, or manners,
You would not make me such an argument.
But fare ye well. 'Tis partly my own fault,
Which death or absence soon shall remedy.

My love, my life, my soul, fair Helena!

sentences.speaker: HELENA

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
[To LYSANDER]
Sweet, do not scorn her so.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: If she cannot entreat, I can compel.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
Thou canst compel no more than she entreat.
Thy threats have no more strength than her weak
prayers.
Helen, I love thee. By my life, I do.
I swear by that which I will lose for thee
To prove him false that says I love thee not.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: [To HERMIA] Away, you Ethiope!

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
No, no. He’ll
Seem to break loose. Take on as you would follow,
But yet come not. You are a tame man, go!

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
[To HERMIA] Hang off, thou cat, thou burr! Vile thing,
let loose
Or I will shake thee from me like a serpent.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
Why are you grown so rude? What change is this,
Sweet love?

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
Why are you grown so rude? What change is this,

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
Thy love? Out, tawny Tartar, out!
Out, loathèd medicine! O hated potion, hence!

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: Do you not jest?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Yes, sooth, and so do you.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: Demetrius, I will keep my word with thee.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
I would I had your bond, for I perceive
A weak bond holds you. I’ll not trust your word.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
What, should I hurt her, strike her, kill her dead?
Although I hate her, I’ll not harm her so.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
What, can you do me greater harm than hate?
Hate me? Wherefore? O me! What news, my love?
Am not I Hermia? Are not you Lysander?
I am as fair now as I was erewhile.
Since night you loved me. Yet since night you left me.
Why then, you left me—Oh, the gods forbid!—
In earnest, shall I say?

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
Ay, by my life,
And never did desire to see thee more.
Therefore be out of hope, of question, of doubt.
Be certain, nothing truer. 'Tis no jest
That I do hate thee and love Helena.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
O me! [To HELENA] You juggler! You canker-blossom!
You thief of love! What, have you come by night
And stol'n my love’s heart from him?

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
O me! [To HELENA] You juggler! You canker-blossom!

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
O me! [To HELENA] You juggler! You canker-blossom!
You thief of love! What, have you come by night
And stol'n my love’s heart from him?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Fine, i' faith!
Have you no modesty, no maiden shame,
No touch of bashfulness? What, will you tear
Impatient answers from my gentle tongue?
Fie, fie! You counterfeit, you puppet, you!

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
“Puppet”? Why so? Ay, that way goes the game.
Now I perceive that she hath made compare
Between our statures. She hath urged her height,
And with her personage, her tall personage,
Her height, forsooth, she hath prevailed with him.
And are you grown so high in his esteem
Because I am so dwarfish and so low?
How low am I, thou painted maypole? Speak.
How low am I? I am not yet so low
But that my nails can reach unto thine eyes.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
[To LYSANDER and DEMETRIUS]
I pray you, though you mock me, gentlemen,
Let her not hurt me. I was never cursed.
I have no gift at all in shrewishness.
I am a right maid for my cowardice.
Let her not strike me. You perhaps may think,
Because she is something lower than myself,
That I can match her.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: “Lower”? Hark, again!

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Good Hermia, do not be so bitter with me.
I evermore did love you, Hermia,
Did ever keep your counsels, never wronged you—
Save that, in love unto Demetrius,
I told him of your stealth unto this wood.
He followed you. For love I followed him.
But he hath chid me hence and threatened me
To strike me, spurn me—nay, to kill me too.
And now, so you will let me quiet go,
To Athens will I bear my folly back
And follow you no further. Let me go.
You see how simple and how fond I am.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: Why, get you gone! Who is ’t that hinders you?

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: Why, get you gone! Who is ’t that hinders you?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: A foolish heart, that I leave here behind.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: What, with Lysander?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: With Demetrius.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: Be not afraid. She shall not harm thee, Helena.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: Be not afraid. She shall not harm thee, Helena.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: No, sir, she shall not, though you take her part.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Oh, when she’s angry, she is keen and shrewd!
She was a vixen when she went to school.
And though she be but little, she is fierce.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
“Little” again? Nothing but “low” and “little”!
Why will you suffer her to flout me thus?
Let me come to her.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
[To HERMIA] Get you gone, you dwarf,
You minimus of hindering knotgrass made,
You bead, you acorn!

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
You are too officious
In her behalf that scorns your services.
Let her alone. Speak not of Helena.
Take not her part. For if thou dost intend
Never so little show of love to her,
Thou shalt aby it.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
Now she holds me not.
Now follow, if thou darest, to try whose right,
Of thine or mine, is most in Helena.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
Now she holds me not.
Now follow, if thou darest, to try whose right,

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: “Follow”? Nay, I’ll go with thee, cheek by jowl.
DEMETRIUS and LYSANDER exit.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
You, mistress, all this coil is long of you.
Nay, go not back.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
You, mistress, all this coil is long of you.
Nay, go not back.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I will not trust you, I,
Nor longer stay in your curst company.
Your hands than mine are quicker for a fray.
My legs are longer though, to run away.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I will not trust you, I,
Nor longer stay in your curst company.
Your hands than mine are quicker for a fray.
My legs are longer though, to run away.
HELENA exits.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: I am amazed and know not what to say.
HERMIA exits.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
[To ROBIN] This is thy negligence. Still thou
mistakest,
Or else committ’st thy knaveries willfully.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
[To ROBIN] This is thy negligence. Still thou
mistakest,
Or else committ’st thy knaveries willfully.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
Believe me, King of Shadows, I mistook.
Did not you tell me I should know the man
By the Athenian garment he had on?
And so far blameless proves my enterprise,
That I have 'nointed an Athenian’s eyes.
And so far am I glad it so did sort,
As this their jangling I esteem a sport.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Thou seest these lovers seek a place to fight.
Hie therefore, Robin, overcast the night.
The starry welkin cover thou anon
With drooping fog as black as Acheron,
And lead these testy rivals so astray
As one come not within another’s way.
Like to Lysander sometime frame thy tongue,
Then stir Demetrius up with bitter wrong.
And sometime rail thou like Demetrius.
And from each other look thou lead them thus,
Till o'er their brows death-counterfeiting sleep
With leaden legs and batty wings doth creep.
[Gives ROBIN another flower]
Then crush this herb into Lysander’s eye,
Whose liquor hath this virtuous property
To take from thence all error with his might
And make his eyeballs roll with wonted sight.
When they next wake, all this derision
Shall seem a dream and fruitless vision.
And back to Athens shall the lovers wend,
With league whose date till death shall never end.
Whiles I in this affair do thee employ,
I’ll to my queen and beg her Indian boy.
And then I will her charmèd eye release
From monster’s view, and all things shall be peace.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
My fairy lord, this must be done with haste.
For night’s swift dragons cut the clouds full fast,
And yonder shines Aurora’s harbinger,
At whose approach, ghosts, wandering here and there,
Troop home to churchyards. Damnèd spirits all,
That in crossways and floods have burial,
Already to their wormy beds are gone.
For fear lest day should look their shames upon,
They willfully themselves exile from light
And must for aye consort with black-browed night.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
But we are spirits of another sort.
I with the morning’s love have oft made sport,
And like a forester the groves may tread
Even till the eastern gate, all fiery red,
Opening on Neptune with fair blessèd beams,
Turns into yellow gold his salt green streams.
But notwithstanding, haste. Make no delay.
We may effect this business yet ere day.

I am feared in field and town.
Goblin, lead them up and down.
Here comes one.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: Where art thou, proud Demetrius? Speak thou now.

Here, villain. Drawn and ready. Where art thou?

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS

Lysander, speak again!
Thou runaway, thou coward, art thou fled?
Speak! In some bush? Where dost thou hide thy head?

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
[As LYSANDER] Thou coward, art thou bragging to the
stars,
Telling the bushes that thou look’st for wars,
And wilt not come? Come, recreant. Come, thou child!
I’ll whip thee with a rod. He is defiled
That draws a sword on thee.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
[As LYSANDER] Follow my voice. We’ll try no manhood
here.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
[As LYSANDER] Follow my voice. We’ll try no manhood
here.
DEMETRIUS and ROBIN exit.
LYSANDER enters.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
He goes before me and still dares me on.
When I come where he calls, then he is gone.
The villain is much lighter-heeled than I.
I followed fast, but faster he did fly,
That fallen am I in dark uneven way,
And here will rest me.
[Lies down]
Come, thou gentle day!
For if but once thou show me thy grey light,
I’ll find Demetrius and revenge this spite.
[Sleeps]

[As LYSANDER]
Ho, ho, ho! Coward, why comest thou not?

sentences.speaker: ROBIN

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Abide me, if thou darest! For well I wot
Thou runn’st before me, shifting every place,
And darest not stand nor look me in the face.
Where art thou now?

Abide me, if thou darest! For well I wot
Thou runn’st before me, shifting every place,
And darest not stand nor look me in the face.
Where art thou now?

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text: [As LYSANDER] Come hither. I am here.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text: [As LYSANDER] Come hither. I am here.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Nay, then, thou mock’st me. Thou shalt buy this dear
If ever I thy face by daylight see.
Now go thy way. Faintness constraineth me
To measure out my length on this cold bed.
By day’s approach look to be visited.
[Lies down and sleeps]

HELENA enters.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
O weary night, O long and tedious night,
Abate thy hours. Shine comforts from the east,
That I may back to Athens by daylight
From these that my poor company detest.
And sleep, that sometimes shuts up sorrow’s eye,
Steal me awhile from mine own company.
[Lies down and sleeps]

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
Yet but three? Come one more.
Two of both kinds make up four.
Here she comes, cursed and sad.
Cupid is a knavish lad
Thus to make poor females mad.

HERMIA enters.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
Never so weary, never so in woe,
Bedabbled with the dew and torn with briers,
I can no further crawl, no further go.
My legs can keep no pace with my desires.
Here will I rest me till the break of day.
Heavens shield Lysander if they mean a fray!
[Lies down and sleeps]

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
On the ground
Sleep sound.
I'll apply
To your eye.
Gentle lover, remedy.
[Squeezes flower juice into LYSANDER's eyes]
When thou wakest,
Thou takest
True delight
In the sight
Of thy former lady's eye.
And the country proverb known—
That every man should take his own—
In your waking shall be shown.
Jack shall have Jill.
Nought shall go ill.
The man shall have his mare again, and all shall be
well.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
[To BOTTOM] Come, sit thee down upon this flowery bed
While I thy amiable cheeks do coy,
And stick musk roses in thy sleek, smooth head,
And kiss thy fair large ears, my gentle joy.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text: Where’s Peaseblossom?

sentences.speaker: PEASEBLOSSOM
sentences.text: Ready.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text: Scratch my head, Peaseblossom. Where’s Monsieur Cobweb?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text: Scratch my head, Peaseblossom. Where’s Monsieur Cobweb?

sentences.speaker: COBWEB
sentences.text: Ready.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Monsieur Cobweb, good monsieur, get you your weapons in
your hand and kill me a red-hipped humble-bee on the
top of a thistle. And, good monsieur, bring me the honey
bag. Do not fret yourself too much in the action,
monsieur. And good monsieur, have a care the honey bag
break not. I would be loath to have you overflown with a
honey bag, signor.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text: Where’s Monsieur Mustardseed?

sentences.speaker: MUSTARDSEED
sentences.text: Ready.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Give me your neaf, Monsieur Mustardseed. Pray you,
leave your courtesy, good monsieur.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Give me your neaf, Monsieur Mustardseed. Pray you,
leave your courtesy, good monsieur.

sentences.speaker: MUSTARDSEED
sentences.text: What’s your will?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Nothing, good monsieur, but to help Cavalery Cobweb to
scratch. I must to the barber’s, monsieur, for methinks
I am marvelous hairy about the face. And I am such a
tender ass, if my hair do but tickle me, I must scratch.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text: What, wilt thou hear some music, my sweet love?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
I have a reasonable good ear in music. Let’s have the
tongs and the bones.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
I have a reasonable good ear in music. Let’s have the
tongs and the bones.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text: Or say, sweet love, what thou desirest to eat.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Truly, a peck of provender. I could munch your good dry
oats. Methinks I have a great desire to a bottle of
hay. Good hay, sweet hay, hath no fellow.

hay. Good hay, sweet hay, hath no fellow.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
I have a venturous fairy that shall seek
The squirrel’s hoard and fetch thee new nuts.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
I had rather have a handful or two of dried peas. But,
I pray you, let none of your people stir me. I have an
exposition of sleep come upon me.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
Sleep thou, and I will wind thee in my arms.
Fairies, be gone, and be all ways away.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
So doth the woodbine the sweet honeysuckle
Gently entwist. The female ivy so
Enrings the barky fingers of the elm.
Oh, how I love thee! How I dote on thee!

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Welcome, good Robin. Seest thou this sweet sight?
Her dotage now I do begin to pity.
For, meeting her of late behind the wood,
Seeking sweet favors from this hateful fool,
I did upbraid her and fall out with her.
For she his hairy temples then had rounded
With a coronet of fresh and fragrant flowers,
And that same dew, which sometime on the buds
Was wont to swell like round and orient pearls,
Stood now within the pretty flowerets' eyes
Like tears that did their own disgrace bewail.
When I had at my pleasure taunted her
And she in mild terms begged my patience,
I then did ask of her her changeling child,
Which straight she gave me and her fairy sent
To bear him to my bower in Fairyland.
And now I have the boy, I will undo
This hateful imperfection of her eyes.
And, gentle Puck, take this transformèd scalp
From off the head of this Athenian swain,
That, he awaking when the other do,
May all to Athens back again repair
And think no more of this night’s accidents
But as the fierce vexation of a dream.
But first I will release the fairy queen.
[Squeezing flower juice into TITANIA's eyes]
Be as thou wast wont to be.
See as thou wast wont to see.
Dian’s bud o'er Cupid’s flower
Hath such force and blessèd power.
Now, my Titania, wake you, my sweet queen.

And she in mild terms begged my patience,
I then did ask of her her changeling child,
Which straight she gave me and her fairy sent
To bear him to my bower in Fairyland.
And now I have the boy, I will undo
This hateful imperfection of her eyes.
And, gentle Puck, take this transformèd scalp
From off the head of this Athenian swain,
That, he awaking when the other do,
May all to Athens back again repair
And think no more of this night’s accidents
But as the fierce vexation of a dream.
But first I will release the fairy queen.
[Squeezing flower juice into TITANIA's eyes]
Be as thou wast wont to be.
See as thou wast wont to see.
Dian’s bud o'er Cupid’s flower
Hath such force and blessèd power.
Now, my Titania, wake you, my sweet queen.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Welcome, good Robin. Seest thou this sweet sight?
Her dotage now I do begin to pity.
For, meeting her of late behind the wood,
Seeking sweet favors from this hateful fool,
I did upbraid her and fall out with her.
For she his hairy temples then had rounded
With a coronet of fresh and fragrant flowers,
And that same dew, which sometime on the buds
Was wont to swell like round and orient pearls,
Stood now within the pretty flowerets' eyes
Like tears that did their own disgrace bewail.
When I had at my pleasure taunted her
And she in mild terms begged my patience,
I then did ask of her her changeling child,
Which straight she gave me and her fairy sent
To bear him to my bower in Fairyland.
And now I have the boy, I will undo
This hateful imperfection of her eyes.

From off the head of this Athenian swain,

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
[Waking] My Oberon, what visions have I seen!
Methought I was enamored of an ass.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
[Waking] My Oberon, what visions have I seen!
Methought I was enamored of an ass.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text: There lies your love.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
How came these things to pass?
Oh, how mine eyes do loathe his visage now!

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Silence awhile. Robin, take off this head.
Titania, music call, and strike more dead
Than common sleep of all these five the sense.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text: Music, ho! Music such as charmeth sleep!

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text: Music, ho! Music such as charmeth sleep!
Music.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
[Taking the ass’ head off BOTTOM]
Now when thou wakest, with thine own fool’s eyes peep.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Sound, music! Come, my queen, take hands with me,
And rock the ground whereon these sleepers be.
[Dances with TITANIA]
Now thou and I are new in amity,
And will tomorrow midnight solemnly
Dance in Duke Theseus' house triumphantly,
And bless it to all fair prosperity.
There shall the pairs of faithful lovers be
Wedded, with Theseus, all in jollity.

Fairy King, attend, and mark.
I do hear the morning lark.

sentences.speaker: ROBIN
sentences.text:
Fairy King, attend, and mark.
I do hear the morning lark.

sentences.speaker: OBERON
sentences.text:
Then, my queen, in silence sad,
Trip we after the night’s shade.
We the globe can compass soon
Swifter than the wandering moon.

sentences.speaker: TITANIA
sentences.text:
Come, my lord, and in our flight
Tell me how it came this night
That I sleeping here was found
With these mortals on the ground.
OBERON, TITANIA, and ROBIN exit.
A hunting horn blows. THESEUS enters with, EGEUS, HIPPOLYTA, and his servants.
A hunting horn blows. THESEUS enters with, EGEUS, HIPPOLYTA, and his servants.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Go, one of you, find out the forester.
For now our observation is performed.
And since we have the vaward of the day,
My love shall hear the music of my hounds.
Uncouple in the western valley. Let them go.
Dispatch, I say, and find the forester.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
We will, fair queen, up to the mountain’s top,
And mark the musical confusion
Of hounds and echo in conjunction.

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text:
I was with Hercules and Cadmus once,
When in a wood of Crete they bayed the bear
With hounds of Sparta. Never did I hear
Such gallant chiding. For, besides the groves,
The skies, the fountains, every region near
Seemed all one mutual cry. I never heard
So musical a discord, such sweet thunder.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
My hounds are bred out of the Spartan kind,
So flewed, so sanded, and their heads are hung
With ears that sweep away the morning dew,
Crook-kneed, and dew-lapped like Thessalian bulls,
Slow in pursuit, but matched in mouth like bells,
Each under each. A cry more tunable
Was never hollaed to, nor cheered with horn,
In Crete, in Sparta, nor in Thessaly.
Judge when you hear.
But, soft! What nymphs are these?

sentences.speaker: EGEUS
sentences.text:
My lord, this is my daughter here asleep.
And this, Lysander. This Demetrius is.
This Helena, old Nedar’s Helena.
I wonder of their being here together.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
No doubt they rose up early to observe
The rite of May, and hearing our intent
Came here in grace our solemnity.
But speak, Egeus. Is not this the day
That Hermia should give answer of her choice?

sentences.speaker: THESEUS

That Hermia should give answer of her choice?

sentences.speaker: EGEUS
sentences.text: It is, my lord.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: Go tell the hunters to wake them by blowing their horns.
One of the servants exits.
Wind, horns, and shouts within. LYSANDER, DEMETRIUS, HELENA, and HERMIA wake and start up.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Good morrow, friends. Saint Valentine is past. Begin
these woodbirds but to couple now?

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: Pardon, my lord.
LYSANDER, DEMETRIUS, HELENA, and HERMIA all kneel.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: I pray you all, stand up.
LYSANDER, DEMETRIUS, HELENA, and HERMIA all stand up.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
[To LYSANDER and DEMETRIUS]
I know you two are rival enemies.
How comes this gentle concord in the world,
That hatred is so far from jealousy
To sleep by hate and fear no enmity?

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
[To LYSANDER and DEMETRIUS]
I know you two are rival enemies.
How comes this gentle concord in the world,
That hatred is so far from jealousy
To sleep by hate and fear no enmity?

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
My lord, I shall reply amazèdly,
Half sleep, half waking. But as yet, I swear,
I cannot truly say how I came here.
But as I think—for truly would I speak,
And now do I bethink me, so it is—
I came with Hermia hither. Our intent
Was to be gone from Athens, where we might,
Without the peril of the Athenian law—

sentences.speaker: EGEUS
sentences.text:
[To THESEUS] Enough, enough, my lord. You have enough!
I beg the law, the law, upon his head.
They would have stol'n away, they would, Demetrius,
Thereby to have defeated you and me,
You of your wife and me of my consent,
Of my consent that she should be your wife.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
[To THESEUS] My lord, fair Helen told me of their
stealth,
Of this their purpose hither to this wood.
And I in fury hither followed them,
Fair Helena in fancy following me.
But, my good lord, I wot not by what power—
But by some power it is—my love to Hermia,
Melted as the snow, seems to me now
As the remembrance of an idle gaud
Which in my childhood I did dote upon.
And all the faith, the virtue of my heart,
The object and the pleasure of mine eye,
Is only Helena. To her, my lord,
Was I betrothed ere I saw Hermia.
But like in sickness did I loathe this food.
But as in health, come to my natural taste,
Now I do wish it, love it, long for it,
And will for evermore be true to it.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Fair lovers, you are fortunately met.
Of this discourse we more will hear anon.
Egeus, I will overbear your will.
For in the temple by and by with us
These couples shall eternally be knit.
And, for the morning now is something worn,
Our purposed hunting shall be set aside.
Away with us to Athens. Three and three,
We’ll hold a feast in great solemnity.
Come, Hippolyta.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
These things seem small and undistinguishable,
Like far-off mountains turnèd into clouds.

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text:
Methinks I see these things with parted eye,
When everything seems double.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
So methinks.
And I have found Demetrius like a jewel,
Mine own, and not mine own.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
So methinks.
And I have found Demetrius like a jewel,
Mine own, and not mine own.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Are you sure
That we are awake? It seems to me
That yet we sleep, we dream. Do not you think
The duke was here, and bid us follow him?

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:

That yet we sleep, we dream. Do not you think
The duke was here, and bid us follow him?

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS

Are you sure
That we are awake? It seems to me
That yet we sleep, we dream. Do not you think

sentences.speaker: HERMIA
sentences.text: Yea, and my father.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: And Hippolyta.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: And he did bid us follow to the temple.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Why then, we are awake. Let’s follow him
And by the way let us recount our dreams.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Why then, we are awake. Let’s follow him
And by the way let us recount our dreams.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS

Why then, we are awake. Let’s follow him
And by the way let us recount our dreams.
LYSANDER, DEMETRIUS, HELENA, and HERMIA exit.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
[Waking] When my cue comes, call me, and I will answer.
My next is "Most fair Pyramus." Heigh-ho! Peter Quince?
Flute the bellows-mender? Snout the tinker? Starveling?
God's my life, stol'n hence, and left me asleep? I have
had a most rare vision. I have had a dream—past the wit
of man to say what dream it was. Man is but an ass if
he go about to expound this dream. Methought I was—there
is no man can tell what. Methought I was, and methought
I had—but man is but a patched fool if he will offer to
say what methought I had. The eye of man hath not
heard, the ear of man hath not seen, man's hand is not
able to taste, his tongue to conceive, nor his heart to
report what my dream was. I will get Peter Quince to
write a ballad of this dream. It shall be called
"Bottom's Dream" because it hath no bottom. And I will
sing it in the latter end of a play before the duke.
Peradventure, to make it more gracious, I shall sing it
at her death.

My next is "Most fair Pyramus." Heigh-ho! Peter Quince?
Flute the bellows-mender? Snout the tinker? Starveling?
God's my life, stol'n hence, and left me asleep? I have
had a most rare vision. I have had a dream—past the wit
of man to say what dream it was. Man is but an ass if
he go about to expound this dream. Methought I was—there
is no man can tell what. Methought I was, and methought
I had—but man is but a patched fool if he will offer to
say what methought I had. The eye of man hath not
heard, the ear of man hath not seen, man's hand is not
able to taste, his tongue to conceive, nor his heart to
report what my dream was. I will get Peter Quince to
write a ballad of this dream. It shall be called
"Bottom's Dream" because it hath no bottom. And I will
sing it in the latter end of a play before the duke.
Peradventure, to make it more gracious, I shall sing it
at her death.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Have you sent to Bottom’s house? Is he come home yet?

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Have you sent to Bottom’s house? Is he come home yet?
QUINCE, FLUTE, SNOUT, and STARVELING enter.

sentences.speaker: STARVELING
sentences.text: He cannot be heard of. Out of doubt he is transported.

sentences.speaker: STARVELING
sentences.text: He cannot be heard of. Out of doubt he is transported.

sentences.speaker: FLUTE
sentences.text:
If he come not, then the play is marred. It goes not
forward. Doth it?

sentences.speaker: FLUTE
sentences.text:
If he come not, then the play is marred. It goes not
forward. Doth it?

sentences.speaker: FLUTE

If he come not, then the play is marred. It goes not

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
It is not possible. You have not a man in all Athens
able to discharge Pyramus but he.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
It is not possible. You have not a man in all Athens
able to discharge Pyramus but he.

sentences.speaker: FLUTE
sentences.text:
No, he hath simply the best wit of any handicraft man
in Athens.

sentences.speaker: FLUTE
sentences.text:
No, he hath simply the best wit of any handicraft man
in Athens.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
Yea, and the best person too. And he is a very paramour
for a sweet voice.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text:
Yea, and the best person too. And he is a very paramour
for a sweet voice.

sentences.speaker: FLUTE
sentences.text:
You must say “paragon.” A “paramour” is, God bless us,
a thing of naught.

sentences.speaker: SNUG
sentences.text:
Masters, the duke is coming from the temple, and there
is two or three lords and ladies more married. If our
sport had gone forward, we had all been made men.

sentences.speaker: SNUG
sentences.text:
Masters, the duke is coming from the temple, and there
is two or three lords and ladies more married. If our
sport had gone forward, we had all been made men.
SNUG enters.

sentences.speaker: FLUTE
sentences.text:
O sweet bully Bottom! Thus hath he lost sixpence a day
during his life. He could not have ’scaped sixpence a
day. An the duke had not given him sixpence a day for
playing Pyramus, I’ll be hanged. He would have deserved
it. Sixpence a day in Pyramus, or nothing.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text: Where are these lads? Where are these hearts?

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text: Where are these lads? Where are these hearts?

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Bottom! O most courageous day! O most happy hour!

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Bottom! O most courageous day! O most happy hour!

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Bottom! O most courageous day! O most happy hour!

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Masters, I am to discourse wonders—but ask me not what,
for if I tell you I am no true Athenian. I will tell
you everything, right as it fell out.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Masters, I am to discourse wonders—but ask me not what,
for if I tell you I am no true Athenian. I will tell
you everything, right as it fell out.

sentences.speaker: QUINCE
sentences.text: Let us hear, sweet Bottom.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
Not a word of me. All that I will tell you is that the
duke hath dined. Get your apparel together, good strings
to your beards, new ribbons to your pumps. Meet
presently at the palace. Every man look o'er his part.
For the short and the long is, our play is preferred. In
any case, let Thisbe have clean linen. And let not him
that plays the lion pair his nails, for they shall hang
out for the lion's claws. And most dear actors, eat no
onions nor garlic, for we are to utter sweet breath. And
I do not doubt but to hear them say, "It is a sweet
comedy." No more words. Away, go away!

Enter THESEUS, HIPPOLYTA, and PHILOSTRATE, with other attendant lords

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text: 'Tis strange, my Theseus, that these lovers speak of.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
More strange than true. I never may believe
These antique fables nor these fairy toys.
Lovers and madmen have such seething brains,
Such shaping fantasies, that apprehend
More than cool reason ever comprehends.
The lunatic, the lover, and the poet
Are of imagination all compact.
One sees more devils than vast hell can hold—
That is the madman. The lover, all as frantic,
Sees Helen’s beauty in a brow of Egypt.
The poet’s eye, in fine frenzy rolling,
Doth glance from heaven to Earth, from Earth to heaven.
And as imagination bodies forth
The forms of things unknown, the poet’s pen
Turns them to shapes and gives to airy nothing
A local habitation and a name.
Such tricks hath strong imagination,
That if it would but apprehend some joy,
It comprehends some bringer of that joy.
Or in the night, imagining some fear,
How easy is a bush supposed a bear!

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text:
But all the story of the night told over,
And all their minds transfigured so together,
More witnesseth than fancy’s images
And grows to something of great constancy,
But, howsoever, strange and admirable.

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Here come the lovers, full of joy and mirth.
Joy, gentle friends! Joy and fresh days of love
Accompany your hearts!

sentences.speaker: THESEUS

The lovers enter: LYSANDER, DEMETRIUS, HELENA, and HERMIA.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
More than to us
Wait in your royal walks, your board, your bed!

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Come now, what masques, what dances shall we have
To wear away this long age of three hours
Between our after-supper and bedtime?
Where is our usual manager of mirth?
What revels are in hand? Is there no play,
To ease the anguish of a torturing hour?
Call Philostrate.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Come now, what masques, what dances shall we have
To wear away this long age of three hours
Between our after-supper and bedtime?
Where is our usual manager of mirth?
What revels are in hand? Is there no play,
To ease the anguish of a torturing hour?
Call Philostrate.

sentences.speaker: PHILOSTRATE
sentences.text: Here, mighty Theseus.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Say, what abridgement have you for this evening?
What masque, what music? How shall we beguile
The lazy time if not with some delight?

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Say, what abridgement have you for this evening?
What masque, what music? How shall we beguile
The lazy time if not with some delight?

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
Say, what abridgement have you for this evening?

sentences.speaker: PHILOSTRATE
sentences.text:
[Giving THESEUS a paper]
There is a brief, how many sports are ripe.
Make choice of which your highness will see first.

sentences.speaker: PHILOSTRATE
sentences.text:
[Giving THESEUS a paper]
There is a brief, how many sports are ripe.
Make choice of which your highness will see first.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
[Reads]
“The battle with the Centaurs, to be sung
By an Athenian eunuch to the harp.”
We’ll none of that. That have I told my love,
In glory of my kinsman Hercules.
“The riot of the tipsy Bacchanals,
Tearing the Thracian singer in their rage.”
That is an old device, and it was played
When I from Thebes came last a conqueror.
“The thrice three Muses mourning for the death
Of learning, late deceased in beggary.”
That is some satire, keen and critical,
Not sorting with a nuptial ceremony.
“A tedious brief scene of young Pyramus
And his love Thisbe. Very tragical mirth.”
“Merry” and “tragical?” “Tedious” and “brief?”
That is hot ice and wondrous strange snow.
How shall we find the concord of this discord?

sentences.speaker: PHILOSTRATE
sentences.text:
A play there is, my lord, some ten words long,
Which is as brief as I have known a play.
But by ten words, my lord, it is too long,
Which makes it tedious. For in all the play
There is not one word apt, one player fitted.
And tragical, my noble lord, it is.
For Pyramus therein doth kill himself.
Which, when I saw rehearsed, I must confess,
Made mine eyes water—but more merry tears
The passion of loud laughter never shed.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: What are they that do play it?

sentences.speaker: PHILOSTRATE
sentences.text:
Hard-handed men that work in Athens here,
Which never labored in their minds till now,
And now have toiled their unbreathed memories
With this same play against your nuptial.

sentences.speaker: PHILOSTRATE
sentences.text:
Hard-handed men that work in Athens here,

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: And we will hear it.

sentences.speaker: PHILOSTRATE
sentences.text:
No, my noble lord.
It is not for you. I have heard it over,
And it is nothing, nothing in the world—
Unless you can find sport in their intents,
Extremely stretched and conned with cru 'l pain
To do you service.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
I will hear that play.
For never anything can be amiss
When simpleness and duty tender it.
Go, bring them in. And take your places, ladies.

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text:
I love not to see wretchedness o'er charged
And duty in his service perishing.
PHILOSTRATE exits.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: Why, gentle sweet, you shall see no such thing.

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text: He says they can do nothing in this kind.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
The kinder we, to give them thanks for nothing.
Our sport shall be to take what they mistake,
And what poor duty cannot do, noble respect
Takes it in might, not merit.
Where I have come, great clerks have purposèd
To greet me with premeditated welcomes,
Where I have seen them shiver and look pale,
Make periods in the midst of sentences,
Throttle their practiced accent in their fears,
And in conclusion dumbly have broke off,
Not paying me a welcome. Trust me, sweet,
Out of this silence yet I picked a welcome,
And in the modesty of fearful duty
I read as much as from the rattling tongue
Of saucy and audacious eloquence.
Love, therefore, and tongue-tied simplicity
In least speak most, to my capacity.

PHILOSTRATE enters.

sentences.speaker: PHILOSTRATE
sentences.text: So please your grace, the Prologue is addressed.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: Let him approach.
QUINCE enters, performing as the PROLOGUE.

sentences.speaker: PROLOGUE
sentences.text:
If we offend, it is with our good will.
That you should think we come not to offend,
But with good will. To show our simple skill,
That is the true beginning of our end.
Consider then we come but in despite.
We do not come as minding to contest you,
Our true intent is. All for your delight
We are not here. That you should here repent you,
The actors are at hand, and by their show
You shall know all that you are like to know.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: This fellow doth not stand upon points.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text:
He hath rid his prologue like a rough colt. He knows
not the stop. A good moral, my lord: it is not enough to
speak, but to speak true.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text:
Indeed he hath played on his prologue like a child on a
recorder—a sound, but not in government.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
His speech was like a tangled chain. Nothing impaired,
but all disordered. Who is next?

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
His speech was like a tangled chain. Nothing impaired,
but all disordered. Who is next?

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
His speech was like a tangled chain. Nothing impaired,
but all disordered. Who is next?
Enter BOTTOM as PYRAMUS; FLUTE as THISBE; SNOUT as WALL; STARVELING as MOONSHINE; and SNUG as LION.

sentences.speaker: PROLOGUE
sentences.text:
Gentles, perchance you wonder at this show.
But wonder on, till truth make all things plain.
This man is Pyramus, if you would know.
This beauteous lady Thisbe is certain.
This man, with lime and roughcast, doth present
Wall, that vile wall which did these lovers sunder.
And through Wall’s chink, poor souls, they are content
To whisper. At the which let no man wonder.
This man, with lanthorn, dog, and bush of thorn,
Presenteth Moonshine. For, if you will know,
By moonshine did these lovers think no scorn
To meet at Ninus' tomb—there, there to woo.
This grisly beast, which “Lion” hight by name,
The trusty Thisbe, coming first by night,
Did scare away, or rather did affright.
And, as she fled, her mantle she did fall,
Which Lion vile with bloody mouth did stain.
Anon comes Pyramus, sweet youth and tall,
And finds his trusty Thisbe’s mantle slain.
Whereat, with blade, with bloody blameful blade,
He bravely broached his boiling bloody breast.
And Thisbe, tarrying in mulberry shade,
His dagger drew, and died. For all the rest,
Let Lion, Moonshine, Wall, and lovers twain
At large discourse, while here they do remain.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: I wonder if the lion be to speak.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: No wonder, my lord. One lion may when many asses do.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: No wonder, my lord. One lion may when many asses do.

sentences.speaker: WALL
sentences.text:
In this same interlude it doth befall
That I, one Snout by name, present a wall.
And such a wall, as I would have you think,
That had in it a crannied hole, or chink,
Through which the lovers, Pyramus and Thisbe,
Did whisper often very secretly.
This loam, this roughcast, and this stone doth show
That I am that same wall. The truth is so.
And this the cranny is, right and sinister,
Through which the fearful lovers are to whisper.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: Would you desire lime and hair to speak better?

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
It is the wittiest partition that ever I heard
discourse, my lord.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: Pyramus draws near the wall. Silence!

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: Pyramus draws near the wall. Silence!
PYRAMUS enters.

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text:
O grim-looked night! O night with hue so black!
O night, which ever art when day is not!
O night, O night! Alack, alack, alack,
I fear my Thisbe’s promise is forgot!
And thou, O Wall, O sweet, O lovely Wall,
That stand’st between her father’s ground and mine.
Thou Wall, O Wall, O sweet and lovely Wall,
Show me thy chink to blink through with mine eyne!

That stand’st between her father’s ground and mine.
Thou Wall, O Wall, O sweet and lovely Wall,
Show me thy chink to blink through with mine eyne!

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text:
O grim-looked night! O night with hue so black!
O night, which ever art when day is not!
O night, O night! Alack, alack, alack,
I fear my Thisbe’s promise is forgot!
And thou, O Wall, O sweet, O lovely Wall,
That stand’st between her father’s ground and mine.
Thou Wall, O Wall, O sweet and lovely Wall,
Show me thy chink to blink through with mine eyne!

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: The wall, methinks, being sensible, should curse again.

sentences.speaker: BOTTOM
sentences.text:
No, in truth, sir, he should not. “Deceiving me” is
Thisbe’s cue. She is to enter now and I am to spy her
through the wall. You shall see, it will fall pat as I
told you. Yonder she comes.

sentences.speaker: THISBE
sentences.text:
O Wall, full often hast thou heard my moans,
For parting my fair Pyramus and me!
My cherry lips have often kissed thy stones,
Thy stones with lime and hair knit up in thee.

sentences.speaker: THISBE
sentences.text:
O Wall, full often hast thou heard my moans,
For parting my fair Pyramus and me!
My cherry lips have often kissed thy stones,
Thy stones with lime and hair knit up in thee.

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text:
I see a voice. Now will I to the chink,
To spy an I can hear my Thisbe’s face. Thisbe?

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text:
I see a voice. Now will I to the chink,
To spy an I can hear my Thisbe’s face. Thisbe?

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text:
I see a voice. Now will I to the chink,
To spy an I can hear my Thisbe’s face. Thisbe?

sentences.speaker: THISBE
sentences.text: My love thou art, my love, I think.

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text:
Think what thou wilt, I am thy lover’s grace.
And like Limander am I trusty still.

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text:
Think what thou wilt, I am thy lover’s grace.
And like Limander am I trusty still.

sentences.speaker: THISBE
sentences.text: And I like Helen, till the Fates me kill.

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text: Not Shafalus to Procrus was so true.

sentences.speaker: THISBE
sentences.text: As Shafalus to Procrus, I to you.

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text: Oh, kiss me through the hole of this vile wall!

sentences.speaker: THISBE
sentences.text: I kiss the wall’s hole, not your lips at all.

sentences.speaker: PYRAMUS
sentences.text: Wilt thou at Ninny’s tomb meet me straightway?

sentences.speaker: THISBE
sentences.text: Tide life, tide death, I come without delay.
PYRAMUS and THISBE exit.

sentences.speaker: WALL
sentences.text:
Thus have I, Wall, my part dischargèd so.
And, being done, thus Wall away doth go.

sentences.speaker: WALL
sentences.text:
Thus have I, Wall, my part dischargèd so.
And, being done, thus Wall away doth go.
WALL exits.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: Now is the moon down between the two neighbors.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
No remedy, my lord, when walls are so willful to hear
without warning.

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text: This is the silliest stuff that ever I heard.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
The best in this kind are but shadows, and the worst
are no worse if imagination amend them.

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text: It must be your imagination then, and not theirs.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
If we imagine no worse of them than they of themselves,
they may pass for excellent men. Here come two noble
beasts in, a man and a lion.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
If we imagine no worse of them than they of themselves,
they may pass for excellent men. Here come two noble
beasts in, a man and a lion.

sentences.speaker: LION
sentences.text:
You, ladies, you whose gentle hearts do fear
The smallest monstrous mouse that creeps on floor,
May now perchance both quake and tremble here,
When lion rough in wildest rage doth roar.
Then know that I, as Snug the joiner, am
A lion fell, nor else no lion’s dam.
For if I should as lion come in strife
Into this place, ’twere pity on my life.

sentences.speaker: LION
sentences.text:

Then know that I, as Snug the joiner, am
A lion fell, nor else no lion’s dam.
For if I should as lion come in strife
Into this place, ’twere pity on my life.
LION and MOONSHINE enter.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: A very gentle beast, of a good conscience.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: A very best at a beast, my lord, that e'er I saw.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: This lion is a very fox for his valor.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: True. And a goose for his discretion.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text: True. And a goose for his discretion.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Not so, my lord. For his valor cannot carry his
discretion, and the fox carries the goose.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Not so, my lord. For his valor cannot carry his
discretion, and the fox carries the goose.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
His discretion, I am sure, cannot carry his valor, for
the goose carries not the fox. It is well. Leave it to
his discretion, and let us listen to the moon.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:

the goose carries not the fox. It is well. Leave it to
his discretion, and let us listen to the moon.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:

the goose carries not the fox. It is well. Leave it to
his discretion, and let us listen to the moon.

sentences.speaker: MOONSHINE
sentences.text: This lanthorn doth the hornèd moon present—

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text: He should have worn the horns on his head.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
He is no crescent, and his horns are invisible within
the circumference.

sentences.speaker: MOONSHINE
sentences.text:
This lanthorn doth the hornèd moon present.
Myself the man i' th' moon do seem to be.

sentences.speaker: MOONSHINE
sentences.text:
This lanthorn doth the hornèd moon present.
Myself the man i' th' moon do seem to be.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
This is the greatest error of all the rest. The man
should be put into the lanthorn. How is it else the “man
i' th' moon?”

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
This is the greatest error of all the rest. The man
should be put into the lanthorn. How is it else the “man
i' th' moon?”

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
This is the greatest error of all the rest. The man
should be put into the lanthorn. How is it else the “man
i' th' moon?”

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
He dares not come there for the candle. For you see, it
is already in snuff.

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
He dares not come there for the candle. For you see, it
is already in snuff.

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text: I am aweary of this moon. Would he would change!

sentences.speaker: HIPPOLYTA
sentences.text: I am aweary of this moon. Would he would change!

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
It appears by his small light of discretion, that he is
in the wane. But yet, in courtesy, in all reason, we
must stay the time.

sentences.speaker: THESEUS
sentences.text:
It appears by his small light of discretion, that he is
in the wane. But yet, in courtesy, in all reason, we
must stay the time.

sentences.speaker: LYSANDER
sentences.text: Proceed, Moon.

sentences.speaker: MOONSHINE
sentences.text:
All that I have to say is to tell you that the lanthorn
is the moon; I, the man in the moon; this thornbush, my
thornbush; and this dog, my dog.

sentences.speaker: MOONSHINE
